translation: He was despised and rejected by men; a man of sorrows, and acquainted with grief. And as one from whom people hide their faces, he was despised—so we did not esteem him.

alternate_translations.translation: He was looked down on and avoided by people: a man full of suffering, bearing the burden of grief. People kept their faces averted from him; and so he was treated with contempt, and we did not value him.
alternate_translations.notes: “Despised” can also be rendered as “looked down on/treated with contempt.” “With people hide their faces” can be expressed idiomatically as “kept their faces averted.” “We did not esteem him” naturally becomes “we did not value him.”

notes: Key nuance: וַחֲדַ֣ל אִישִׁ֔ים (“and [one who] ceases/has his place removed among men”) is commonly understood in Isaiah 53 as “rejected/left out of society.” The phrase כְּמַסְתֵּר פָּנִים מִמֶּנּוּ (“as one from whom faces are hidden from him”) conveys social disgrace—people avert their gaze. The closing line וְלֹ֥א חֲשַׁבְנֻֽהוּ means “we did not think/consider/esteem him,” i.e., the community failed to recognize his worth.
